findings.title: Anyone can replace the registry and grant themselves mint authority

best_agent_match.title: Anyone can replace the registry and grant themselves unlimited minting rights

findings.title: Configured `maxSupply` is never enforced, so the token cap is meaningless

best_agent_match.title: The declared `maxSupply` cap is never enforced, so BBT can exceed its supposed hard cap

findings.title: The proxy deployment would be first-caller capturable if it was deployed without initialization calldata

best_agent_match.title: Uninitialized upgradeable deployments can be captured by the first caller

rejected_candidates.title: Hardcoded Owner Address Creates Single Point of Failure
rejected_candidates.reason: This is a centralization concern, not a standalone exploitable bug, and in this codebase `maxSupply` is never enforced anyway, so controlling `setMaxSupply()` does not directly affect token behavior.

rejected_candidates.title: Token Name and Symbol Mismatch with ERC20 Standard
rejected_candidates.reason: This is a metadata inconsistency, not a protocol-level security issue causing fund loss, insolvency, lockup, or realistic DoS.

rejected_candidates.title: Registry getContractAddress Reverts Instead of Returning Safe Default
rejected_candidates.reason: Failing closed on unknown keys is expected behavior. Any mint DoS from a bad registry is already subsumed by the unrestricted `setRegistry()` issue; without that, this mainly reflects owner misconfiguration rather than an independent exploit.
